From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

University committed to research as a central part of its mission

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Sir_Thomas_Lawrence_%281769-1830%29_-_Charles_William%2C_Baron_von_Humboldt_%281767-1835%29_-_RCIN_404936_-_Royal_Collection.jpg/250px-Sir_Thomas_Lawrence_%281769-1830%29_-_Charles_William%2C_Baron_von_Humboldt_%281767-1835%29_-_RCIN_404936_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Thomas_Lawrence_(1769-1830)_-_Charles_William,_Baron_von_Humboldt_(1767-1835)_-_RCIN_404936_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)

[Wilhelm von Humboldt](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Humboldt "Wilhelm von Humboldt"), credited with the [Humboldtian model of higher education](/wiki/Humboldtian_model_of_higher_education "Humboldtian model of higher education")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Interior_of_the_Hutzler_Reading_room_located_in_Gilman_Hall%2C_on_the_Johns_Hopkins_University_campus_in_Baltimore%2C_Maryland_LCCN2013646484.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Interior_of_the_Hutzler_Reading_room_located_in_Gilman_Hall%2C_on_the_Johns_Hopkins_University_campus_in_Baltimore%2C_Maryland_LCCN2013646484.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interior_of_the_Hutzler_Reading_room_located_in_Gilman_Hall,_on_the_Johns_Hopkins_University_campus_in_Baltimore,_Maryland_LCCN2013646484.tif)

[Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University") in [Baltimore, Maryland](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") (Gilman Hall pictured), founded in 1876, is considered the first research university in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")[[1]](#cite_note-Geiger_Page_8-1) and as of fiscal year 2020 had been the national leader in annual research and development spending for over four decades.[[2]](#cite_note-June-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Universities_Use_Nuclear_Materials_in_Lab_Experiments_%287845753974%29.jpg/250px-Universities_Use_Nuclear_Materials_in_Lab_Experiments_%287845753974%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Universities_Use_Nuclear_Materials_in_Lab_Experiments_(7845753974).jpg)

Nuclear research at the [University of Wisconsin–Madison](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Madison "University of Wisconsin–Madison"), a research university, in [Madison, Wisconsin](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin")

A **research university** or a **research-intensive university** is a [university](/wiki/University "University") that is committed to [research](/wiki/Research "Research") as a central part of its mission.[[3]](#cite_note-Role-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Altbach-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Taylor-6) They are "the key sites of [knowledge production](/wiki/Knowledge_production_modes "Knowledge production modes")", along with "intergenerational [knowledge transfer](/wiki/Knowledge_transfer "Knowledge transfer") and the certification of new knowledge" through the awarding of [doctoral degrees](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy"), and continue to be "the very center of scientific productivity".[[7]](#cite_note-Powell-7) They can be [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") or [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university"), and often have well-known brand names.[[8]](#cite_note-CollegeSolution-8)

Undergraduate courses at many research universities are often academic rather than [vocational](/wiki/Vocational_university "Vocational university") and may not prepare students for particular careers, but many employers value degrees from research universities because they teach fundamental [life skills](/wiki/Life_skills "Life skills") such as [critical thinking](/wiki/Critical_thinking "Critical thinking").[[9]](#cite_note-Andreatta2-9) Globally, research universities are overwhelmingly [public institutions](/wiki/Public_universities "Public universities"), while some countries like the United States and Japan also have well-known [private](/wiki/Private_universities "Private universities") research institutions.[[3]](#cite_note-Role-3)

Institutions of higher education that are not research universities or do not aspire to that designation, such as [liberal arts colleges](/wiki/Liberal_arts_college "Liberal arts college"), instead place more emphasis on student instruction or other aspects of tertiary education, whereas research university faculty members, in contrast, are under more pressure to [publish or perish](/wiki/Publish_or_perish "Publish or perish").[[10]](#cite_note-Irons-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Research_university&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Research_university&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 19th century")]

The concept of the research university first arose in early 19th-century [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") in Germany, where [Wilhelm von Humboldt](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Humboldt "Wilhelm von Humboldt") championed his vision of *Einheit von Lehre und Forschung* (the unity of teaching and research), as a means of producing an education that focused on the main areas of knowledge, including the natural sciences, social sciences, and [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), rather than on the previous goals of the university education, which was to develop an understanding of truth, beauty, and [goodness](/wiki/Good "Good").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Menand-12)

[Roger L. Geiger](/wiki/Roger_L._Geiger "Roger L. Geiger"), "the leading historian of the American research university,"[[13]](#cite_note-Macintyre_Page_333-13) has argued that "the model for the American research university was established by five of the nine [colonial colleges](/wiki/Colonial_colleges "Colonial colleges") chartered before the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") ([Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania"), [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"), and [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University")); five state universities ([Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Madison "University of Wisconsin–Madison"), [Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota"), [Illinois](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_at_Urbana%E2%80%93Champaign "University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign"), and [California](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley")); and five private institutions conceived from their inception as research universities ([MIT](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology"), [Cornell](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"), [Johns Hopkins](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University"), [Stanford](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"), and [Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"))."[[14]](#cite_note-Crow-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Geiger_Page_3-15) The American research university first emerged in the late 19th century, when these fifteen institutions began to graft graduate programs derived from the German model onto undergraduate programs derived from the British model.[[14]](#cite_note-Crow-14) At Johns Hopkins, president [Daniel Coit Gilman](/wiki/Daniel_Coit_Gilman "Daniel Coit Gilman") led the development of the American research university[[1]](#cite_note-Geiger_Page_8-1) by setting high standards for recruiting faculty and admitting students, and insisting that faculty members had to commit to both teaching and research.[[16]](#cite_note-Benson_Page_x-16)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Research_university&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Research universities were essential to the establishment of American [hegemony](/wiki/Hegemony "Hegemony") by the end of the 20th century.[[17]](#cite_note-Marginson_Page_67-17) Most importantly, Berkeley, Chicago, Columbia, and Princeton (along with Birmingham and Cambridge in the UK) directly participated in the creation of the first [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon") (the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project")).[[18]](#cite_note-Herken_Page_48-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Reed_Page_122-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Besides that, Columbia and Harvard were instrumental in the early development of the [American film industry](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") (Hollywood),[[21]](#cite_note-Decherney-21) MIT and Stanford were leaders in building the American [military–industrial complex](/wiki/Military%E2%80%93industrial_complex "Military–industrial complex")[[22]](#cite_note-22) and developing [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"),[[23]](#cite_note-Boden_Page_734-23) and Berkeley and Stanford played a central role in the development of [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley").[[24]](#cite_note-24) The "most prestigious group of research universities" in the United States is the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities").[[25]](#cite_note-Smith_Page_78-25)

Since the 1960s, American research universities, especially the leading American public research [university system](/wiki/University_system "University system"), the [University of California](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California"),[[4]](#cite_note-Altbach-4)[[26]](#cite_note-Kerr_Page_413-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Marginson-27) have served as models for research universities around the world.[[28]](#cite_note-Graham-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Vest_Page_7-29) Having one or more universities based on the American model (including the use of [English as a lingua franca](/wiki/English_as_a_lingua_franca "English as a lingua franca")) is a badge of "social progress and [modernity](/wiki/Modernity "Modernity")" for the contemporary [nation-state](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state").[[30]](#cite_note-Stevens_Page_129-30)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Research_university&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 21st century")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Research_spending_gdp%2C_World%2C_2023_%28cropped%29.svg/500px-Research_spending_gdp%2C_World%2C_2023_%28cropped%29.svg.png)

Research spending gdp

The Americans' continued dominance into the early 21st century has forced their European counterparts to confront the urgent need for reform to avoid "declining into an advanced form of feeder colleges for the best American universities."[[31]](#cite_note-Wittrock-31)

During that same timeframe, several wealthy [petrostates](/wiki/Petrostate "Petrostate") in the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") region subsidized the creation of local branches of American universities.[[32]](#cite_note-Economist-32)

When that approach proved insufficient to establish indigenous [research](/wiki/Knowledge_ecosystem "Knowledge ecosystem") and [startup ecosystems](/wiki/Startup_ecosystem "Startup ecosystem") (to support the planned long-term diversification of their economies away from [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum")), they started to build their own research universities from the ground up by recruiting Western-trained faculty and staff.[[32]](#cite_note-Economist-32)

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Research_university&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

John Taylor, Professor of Higher Education Management at the [University of Liverpool](/wiki/University_of_Liverpool "University of Liverpool"), defines the key characteristics of successful research universities as:[[6]](#cite_note-Taylor-6)

* "Presence of pure and applied research"
* "Delivery of research-led teaching"
* "Breadth of [academic disciplines](/wiki/Academic_discipline "Academic discipline")"
* "High proportion of [postgraduate research](/wiki/Postgraduate_research "Postgraduate research") programmes"
* "High levels of external income"
* "An international perspective"

Philip Altbach defines a different, although similar, set of key characteristics for what research universities need to become successful:[[33]](#cite_note-33)

* At the top of the academic hierarchy in a differentiated higher education system and receiving appropriate support
* Overwhelmingly [public institutions](/wiki/Public_university "Public university")
* Little competition from non-university research institutions, unless these have strong connections to the universities
* More funding than other universities to attract the best staff and students and support research infrastructure
* Adequate and sustained budgets
* Potential for income generation from student fees and intellectual property
* Suitable facilities
* Autonomy
* [Academic freedom](/wiki/Academic_freedom "Academic freedom")

A 2012 [National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine](/wiki/National_Academies_of_Sciences,_Engineering,_and_Medicine "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine") report defined research universities, in the American context, as having values of intellectual freedom, initiative and creativity, excellence, and openness, with such additional characteristics as:[[34]](#cite_note-34)

* Being large and comprehensive – [Clark Kerr](/wiki/Clark_Kerr "Clark Kerr")'s "multiversity"
* Emphasizing the undergraduate residential experience (flagged specifically as distinguishing American research universities from those in continental Europe)
* Integrating [graduate education](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education") with research
* Having faculty engaged in research and scholarship
* Conducting research at high levels
* Having enlightened and bold leadership

Global [university rankings](/wiki/College_and_university_rankings#Global_rankings "College and university rankings") use metrics that primarily measure research to rank universities.[[35]](#cite_note-IHE_2010-35)[[36]](#cite_note-HEPI-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Some also have criteria for inclusion based on the concept of a research university such as teaching at both undergraduate and postgraduate level and conducting work in multiple faculties (*[QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")*),[[38]](#cite_note-38) or teaching undergraduates, having a research output of more than 1,000 research papers over 5 years, and no more than 80% of activity in a single subject area (*[Times Higher Education World University Rankings](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings"))*.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The [Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") in the United States designates institutions that spend on average at least $2.5 million on research and development annually as 'research universities and colleges', with further designations of 'research 2: high spending and doctorate production' and 'research 1: very high spending and doctorate production' for institutions spending over $5 million and awarding 20 or more doctorates per year and institutions spending over $50 million and awarding 70 or more doctorates per year respectively.[[40]](#cite_note-40) As of 2025,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Research_university&action=edit) there were 187 R1 universities, 139 R2 universities and 216 other research universities in the US out of 3,941 total institutions classified.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## Worldwide distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Research_university&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Worldwide distribution")]

The *[QS World University Ranking](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")* for 2021 included 1,002 research universities. The region with the highest number was [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), with 39.8%, followed by [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia")/Pacific with 26.7%, the [US](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") with 15.6%, [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") with 10.8%, and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") and [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") with 7%. All regions except the Middle East and Africa were represented in the top 100. The largest number of new entrants to the rankings were from [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), followed by [Southern Europe](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe").[[42]](#cite_note-42) By individual country, the US had the most institutions with 151, followed by the UK with 84, China with 51 and Germany with 45. The top 200 showed a similar pattern with the US having 45 universities, the UK 26 and Germany 12.[[43]](#cite_note-43) By comparison, the [Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") (2015) identified 115 US universities as "Doctoral Universities: Highest Research Activity" and a further 107 as "Doctoral Universities: Higher Research Activity", while Altbach estimated that there were around 220 research universities in the US in 2013.[[3]](#cite_note-Role-3)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The *[Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities")* for 2020 showed a similar distribution, with 185 of their 500 ranked institutions coming from Europe, 161 from the Americas, 149 from Asia/Oceania and five from Africa. All regions except Africa were represented in the top 100, although the Americas were represented solely by universities from the United States and Canada.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In 2025, China had the most universities in the top 500, 113, followed by the US with 111, the UK with 37 and Germany with 35.[[46]](#cite_note-46) However, the top 200 shows the different pattern: the US with 58 followed by China with 39 and the UK with 18.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The 2026 *[Times Higher Education](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings")* only gave a breakdown by country and only for its top 200; this again had the US at the top with 55, followed by the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") with 26, [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") with 18 and [China](/wiki/China "China") with 13. The top 200 featured one university from Africa, the [University of Cape Town](/wiki/University_of_Cape_Town "University of Cape Town") in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), but none from [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").[[48]](#cite_note-48) The *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* Best Global Universities Ranking 2025 gave numbers by country for the 2250 universities ranked from more than 100 countries: China was the top, with 397, followed by the US with 280 and India with 118.[[49]](#cite_note-49) However, the U.S. had 115 schools in the top 500, followed by China with 70 schools and the UK with 37.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The 2025 *[CWTS Leiden Ranking](/wiki/CWTS_Leiden_Ranking "CWTS Leiden Ranking")* included 1,594 universities in the rankings from 77 countries/regions: China topped the list, with 356, followed by the US with 204 and the UK with 61.[[51]](#cite_note-51)
